subtopic: Private Space Sector and Solar Observatories
title: 3I/ATLAS
focus: Interstellar comet discovered by NASA-funded ATLAS telescope
rationale: This is a brief, distinct topic focusing on a specific astronomical discovery and its orbital characteristics.
sectionExcerpt: NASA funded ATLAS (Asteroid Terrestrial-impact Last Alert System) survey telescope in Chile discovered an interstellar Comet named 3I/ATLAS. It is an active comet categorized as interstellar because of the hyperbolic shape of its orbital path. It does not follow a closed orbital path about the Sun.
evidenceBullets: Discovered by the NASA funded ATLAS survey telescope located in Chile.
Categorized as interstellar due to the hyperbolic shape of its orbital path.
Does not follow a closed orbital path about the Sun.

subtopic: Private Space Sector and Solar Observatories
title: Kodaikanal Solar Observatory
focus: Historical solar observatory, its location, operations, and the Evershed Effect
rationale: This section contains multiple testable facts for UPSC, including the operating institute, geographical location, and the scientific phenomenon known as the Evershed Effect.
sectionExcerpt: Researchers from the Aryabhatta Research Institute of Observational Sciences (ARIES) tapped the century old data from the Kodaikanal Solar Observatory to reconstruct the Sun's past polar magnetic behaviour. Operated by the Indian Institute of Astrophysics (an autonomous institute of DST), it is located in the Palani Range of hills in Southern India. It is known for the discovery of the Evershed Effect, which is a nearly horizontal outflow of gases observed in the penumbrae of sunspots in the photospheric layer.
evidenceBullets: Established in 1899 and operated by the Indian Institute of Astrophysics, an autonomous institute of DST.
Located in the Palani Range of hills in Southern India.
Known for the discovery of the Evershed Effect.
The Evershed Effect is a nearly horizontal outflow of gases observed in the penumbrae of sunspots in the photospheric layer (visible surface of the Sun).

subtopic: Private Space Sector and Solar Observatories
title: Vikram-1
focus: India's private orbital-class launch vehicle developed by Skyroot Aerospace
rationale: Covers a significant development in India's private space sector with specific technical details such as rocket stages, fuel types, and structural materials.
sectionExcerpt: Prime Minister unveiled Vikram-1, which is India's new private orbital-class launch vehicle. It is developed by Skyroot Aerospace, a private Indian space-startup based in Hyderabad. Its purpose is to launch satellites into LEO or SSO, serving small satellites and rideshare missions. It is a four-stage rocket where the first three stages use solid-fuel boosters, and the final stage uses a liquid-propellant Raman engine. The rocket uses an all-carbon-fibre body and features 3D-printed engines.
evidenceBullets: Vikram-1 is India's new private orbital-class launch vehicle developed by Skyroot Aerospace.
Designed to launch small satellites and rideshare missions into Low Earth Orbit (LEO) or Sun-Synchronous Orbit (SSO).
It is a four-stage rocket: the first three stages use solid-fuel boosters.
The final (upper) stage uses a liquid-propellant Raman engine for precise orbital insertion.
Features an all-carbon-fibre body and 3D-printed engines for a lightweight, efficient design.
